text: laparoscopic stricturoplasty as a treatment of stenosing Crohn's disease. The role of surgery in Crohn's disease has changed in recent years due to the development of new effective treatments. However, more than eighty percent of Crohn's disease patients require surgery at least once in their life. Decisions are usually made in multidisciplinary committees. Medical treatment failure and complications are the main indications of surgery. Nowadays, alloporescopic approach is the treatment of choice. The main objective is the intestinal preservation and it should be limited to macroscopic injuries that produce the symptoms. Strict xeroplasty is the alternative to resection and it's indicated when a resection causes a short intestinal syndrome.

In this video, we present the case of a thirty-two-year-old man who has suffered from Crohn's disease since two thousand and seven with jejunal involvement, just distal to the trait's angle, and ileocolic affectation. He developed corticodependence, and since that time he has received azathioprine, adalimumab and infliximab, all of them removed by activity persistence. The last one was ustekinumab, every eight weeks, but treatment was stopped when he was admitted due to intestinal obstruction. In the last magnetic resonance enterography is observed a segmental stenosis at proximal jejunum, with a severe dilatation of the duodenum and the stomach. So, due to the medical failure with three biologic drugs and the extraction clinic that the patient suffered from, the multidisciplinary committee decided to carry out the surgery that we saw in this video.

The patient is positioned supine with legs open, and four trocars are inserted: two 12mm and two 5mm. The surgeon performs a Heineken-Mikulich technique stricturoplasty, observing significant gastric dilatation. An asogastric tube is inserted. The dilated trach angle and proximal stenotic jejunum, measuring over seven centimeters, are identified. A meticulous review of the intestinal package is conducted to detect stenosis areas and discard fistulas and abscesses. Macroscopic damaged areas are marked with polyglycolic stitches for later treatment.

The surgeon observes the subserosal adipose tissue surrounding the intestine, known as fat wrapping, and checks the entire intestine, including the ileocecal valve, which appears non-inflamed. The main stenosis causing intestinal obstruction is identified at the proximal jejunum. Using an electric scalpel, a lengthwise incision is made in the anti-mesenteric phase along the stenosis.

The surgeon checks the full opening of the stenotic segment after the left waist section. A transversal closure is performed in a single plane using a continuous absorbable polydeoxanone suture (PDS-III-O). The mucosa shows the classic cobblestone appearance with lengthwise and transverse ulcers, characteristic of advanced Crohn's disease. Two sutures are used due to the long length of the stenosis, visible in the center.

The surgeon examines the entire small bowel to identify stenotic areas, using bulldog laparoscopic forceps to clamp the bowel and prevent contamination before performing enterotomy. The stricturoplasty technique is applied to five reference areas as an alternative to intestinal resection, addressing multiple diffuse injuries in the patient.

the postoperative period was uneventful and the patient was discharged at the fourth postoperative day. The majority of the patients with Crohn's disease manifest predominantly inflammatory phenotype, but they could also initially present as stricturing, fistulizing or combination. Up to one third of patients with Crohn's disease develop strictures within ten years after diagnosis and can occur anywhere along the gastrointestinal tract. Medical therapy has been used as first line approach for the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease. However, its role is limited in structuring phenotype, especially in later stages when fibrous tissue predominates. In addition, the use of corticosteroids and biologics are associated with increased postoperative complications. There are many algorithms for treating Crohn's disease, but this from a recent review is very representative. In these patients, corticosteroids, immunomodulators and up to three different biological drugs had been tested without clinical response. The time of surgery should have been earlier.

The development of techniques that reduce the surgical aggression has been one of the greatest advances in surgery. A laparoscopic approach means fewer additions, a lower surgical infection rate and less risk of incisional hernia. Patients recover bowel function faster, have a shorter hospital stay and suffer fewer complications. There are no differences in the recurrence or morbidity rates when laparoscopic approach is compared to open approach. The main indications of stricturoplasty are diffuse involvement of small intestine, non-flammonous fibrotic stenosis, fast recurrence of the disease, stenosis in a patient who had previously undergone extensive intestinal resection or who suffered short intestinal syndrome. Strict uroplasty shouldn't be done in colonic stenosis, when there's perforation, hypalbuminemia, multiple stenosis inside a short intestinal segment, stenosis next to resected area.
